key_insight: A body-of-revolution human surrogate combined with fast 2.5-D FEM can realistically predict RF scattering for indoor sensing while staying far cheaper than full 3-D simulation.
engineering_relevance: This enables scalable generation of synthetic RF/DFL training data and practical measurement-calibrated prediction in indoor environments without relying on large measurement campaigns.
method: Construct a human-like body-of-revolution (BoR) surrogate that preserves key scattering behavior versus 3-D bodies
Use a fast 2.5-D finite element method to compute fields for a vertically polarized dipole excitation
Calibrate the background field with measurements to implicitly capture complex indoor propagation effects
evidence_signal: In a typical indoor device-free localization scenario, the approach achieved about 85% prediction accuracy and reproduced spatial RSSI variations observed in measurements.
watch_item: Performance depends on measurement-driven calibration and scenario similarity, so accuracy may drop when the indoor layout or propagation conditions change substantially.
paper_title: A Body-of-Revolution Human Model for RF Sensing with Measurement-Driven Calibration for Indoor Environments
